items.title: City intelligence operating model: operating discovery
items.name: City intelligence operating model: operating discovery
items.summary: Discovery work is testing whether city intelligence operating model can move the linked customer and business KPIs while addressing multimodal interoperability, ticketing consistency, and settlement clarity across city partners.
items.opportunity: Clarify whether this bet is strong enough to scale in the next planning cycle, or whether the organization should narrow scope and protect focus.

linkedInitiatives.description: City intelligence operating model Initiative is the quarter's focused investment to move Policy experiment decision cycle and Public-sector platform ARR, anchored in multimodal interoperability, ticketing consistency, and settlement clarity across city partners and led through City Mobility Intelligence with common planning metrics across parking and transport.. The quarter concentrates on account-based ticketing, multimodal settlement, and partner interoperability across urban mobility systems so the work reads like a realistic OKR-driven investment set for a company with roughly 250 product and technology people.

assignments.how: Leads the discovery framing, evidence synthesis, and recommendation on how far this initiative should scale.
Adds workflow, commercial, and implementation evidence needed to close the main delivery risks.
Adds workflow, commercial, and implementation evidence needed to close the main delivery risks.
Adds workflow, commercial, and implementation evidence needed to close the main delivery risks.

riskFocus.question: Will customers buy the product and/or choose to use it?
riskFocus.hypothesis: Customers will engage more consistently if city intelligence operating model measurably improves policy experiment decision cycle.
evidenceToCollect: Whether target users describe policy experiment decision cycle as a top-priority pain point.
Whether the proposed workflow changes are strong enough to change behavior, not just preference.

riskFocus.question: Will the solution meet the needs of the business?
riskFocus.hypothesis: The investment is worth scaling when it improves public-sector platform arr without creating disproportionate operating cost or support load.
evidenceToCollect: Which economic or operating constraints cap the upside.
What minimum KPI movement would justify continued investment.

riskFocus.question: Can customers figure out how to use it?
riskFocus.hypothesis: Users can complete the redesigned workflow with less guidance and less back-and-forth than the current path.
evidenceToCollect: Where users hesitate in the new flow.
Which messages, states, or transitions remain unclear.

riskFocus.question: Can we build it within given resources and constraints?
riskFocus.hypothesis: The teams can deliver the smallest useful slice inside current architecture and staffing constraints without creating a long tail of one-off work.
evidenceToCollect: What can be standardized versus localized or custom.

plannedActivities: Map passenger drop-offs across account-based ticketing, parking, and public-transport payment flows.
Review partner incidents where entitlement, pricing, or settlement states diverged across systems.
Test whether a smaller set of mobility bundles reduces confusion without lowering conversion.
items.decisionToMake: Decide whether to deepen multimodal packaging in the same cities or first stabilize the operator and settlement backbone.

items.opportunity: Clarify whether this bet is strong enough to scale in the next planning cycle, or whether the organization should narrow scope and protect focus.

linkedInitiatives.description: Parking control tower convergence Initiative is the quarter's focused investment to move Receipts per employee and Business and fleet ARR, anchored in multimodal interoperability, ticketing consistency, and settlement clarity across city partners and led through Parking Configuration & Policy Management with reusable tariff and permit models.. The quarter concentrates on account-based ticketing, multimodal settlement, and partner interoperability across urban mobility systems so the work reads like a realistic OKR-driven investment set for a company with roughly 250 product and technology people.
